growth_records.notes: Table 6, authors' own aerobic batch experiment (n=3). mu reported = 0.232 h-1. td=ln2/mu=0.6931/0.232=2.99 h. qS=mu/Yxs=0.232/48.4=0.004793 mol/gDW/h=4.79 mmol/gDW/h. Substrate conc 25.4 mM (3 g/L) per Methods.

growth_records.cite: Bordel et al. Genome-scale metabolic model of the versatile bacterium Paracoccus denitrificans Pd1222. mSystems (2024). DOI:10.1128/msystems.01077-23. [PMC10878069]

growth_records.medium: Mineral salt medium (MSM) with TE3-Zn trace elements

up.method: OD600 growth on sole carbon source; oxidized by NAD-dependent alcohol dehydrogenase EtgB (Pden_2367) to glycolaldehyde then to glycolate by aldehyde dehydrogenase EtgA (Pden_2366)

growth_records.cite: Minrui Ren et al. NAD-dependent dehydrogenases enable efficient growth of Paracoccus denitrificans on the PET monomer ethylene glycol. Nature Communications (2025). DOI:10.1038/s41467-025-61056-x. [PMC12214560]
growth_records.snippet: The bacterium was growing on all tested concentrations of ethylene glycol, with an optimum growth rate at 400 mM (Fig. 2a). However, growth of P. denitrificans on 400 mM ethylene glycol was still considerably slower than on glycolate or gly
growth_records.notes: Optimum growth condition. Growth rate WAS experimentally measured (n=6 independently grown cultures, Fig 2a) but the numeric mu value is only in the Fig 2a/Source Data file, not in the article text, so growth_rate_per_h left null (no arithmetic possible). Medium named as mineral salt medium (MSM) + TE3-Zn trace elements (Methods) but full component list not given in main text -> medium_composition_stated:false. Text-stated comparator growth rates are CITED from other papers, not measured here: E. coli evolved FucO strain mu=0.1 h-1 (refs 29,30); P. putida engineered with BHAC mu=0.24 h-1 improved to 0.31 h-1 by ALE (ref 12). An increased lag phase on ethylene glycol vs glycolate/glyoxylate was observed.

growth_records.medium: Mineral salt medium (MSM) with TE3-Zn trace elements

up.method: OD600 growth on sole carbon source; glycolate oxidized to glyoxylate by glycolate oxidase, glyoxylate assimilated via the beta-hydroxyaspartate cycle (BHAC)

growth_records.cite: Minrui Ren et al. NAD-dependent dehydrogenases enable efficient growth of Paracoccus denitrificans on the PET monomer ethylene glycol. Nature Communications (2025). DOI:10.1038/s41467-025-61056-x. [PMC12214560]
growth_records.snippet: growth of P. denitrificans on 400 mM ethylene glycol was still considerably slower than on glycolate or glyoxylate (Fig. 2b) ... Growth curves of P. denitrificans on 60 mM glycolate (orange), 60 mM glyoxylate (purple), and 400 mM ethylene g
growth_records.notes: Growth measured (Fig 2b growth curve, n=6) but numeric mu only in Source Data file, not in text -> growth_rate_per_h null. Growth on glycolate is faster than on ethylene glycol (qualitative). Medium components not fully enumerated in main text.

growth_records.medium: Mineral salt medium (MSM) with TE3-Zn trace elements

up.method: OD600 growth on sole carbon source; glyoxylate assimilated via the beta-hydroxyaspartate cycle (BHAC), two glyoxylate -> oxaloacetate without CO2 release

growth_records.cite: Minrui Ren et al. NAD-dependent dehydrogenases enable efficient growth of Paracoccus denitrificans on the PET monomer ethylene glycol. Nature Communications (2025). DOI:10.1038/s41467-025-61056-x. [PMC12214560]
growth_records.snippet: growth of P. denitrificans on 400 mM ethylene glycol was still considerably slower than on glycolate or glyoxylate (Fig. 2b).
growth_records.notes: Growth measured (Fig 2b growth curve, n=6) but numeric mu only in Source Data file -> null. Fastest of the three carbon sources tested (glyoxylate > glycolate > ethylene glycol, qualitative). Used as the reference condition in the whole-cell shotgun proteomics comparison vs ethylene glycol.

up.method: anaerobic growth on ethylene glycol as sole carbon source
120 mM KNO3 supplied as terminal electron acceptor for denitrification under argon

growth_records.cite: Minrui Ren et al. NAD-dependent dehydrogenases enable efficient growth of Paracoccus denitrificans on the PET monomer ethylene glycol. Nature Communications (2025). DOI:10.1038/s41467-025-61056-x. [PMC12214560]
growth_records.snippet: P. denitrificans WT and delta-etgC were grown in sealed vials anaerobically at 30 C under an argon atmosphere in minimal medium containing 30 mM ethylene glycol and 120 mM KNO3.
growth_records.notes: Distinct anaerobic (denitrifying) condition. Growth demonstrated but no numeric mu, uptake or secretion rate reported in text (figure/source-data only). Nitrate (120 mM) is the anaerobic electron acceptor. Medium components not fully enumerated in main text.

growth_records.medium: mineral salt medium (with TE3-Zn trace elements)

up.method: LC-MS; biomass-specific uptake rate calculated (Fig. 8c, WT vs dcceR)

growth_records.cite: Lennart Schada von Borzyskowski et al. Multiple levels of transcriptional regulation control glycolate metabolism in Paracoccus denitrificans. mBio (2024). DOI:10.1128/mbio.01524-24. [PMC11323563]
growth_records.snippet: Growth on glycolate (mu = 0.51 h-1) was faster than growth on glyoxylate (0.28 h-1), whereas the growth rates on pyruvate (mu = 0.45 h-1) and glucose (mu = 0.38 h-1) were between these two values
growth_records.notes: WT mu=0.51 h-1 on 60 mM glycolate. td=0.693/0.51=1.36 h. Biomass-specific glycolate uptake rate was measured by LC-MS and shown in Fig. 8c but numeric value not given in text (image only), so rate=null. Medium = mineral salt medium with TE3-Zn trace elements (full recipe referenced, not tabulated -> medium_composition_stated:false). Aerobic (facultative organism).

growth_records.medium: mineral salt medium (with TE3-Zn trace elements)
growth_records.cite: Lennart Schada von Borzyskowski et al. Multiple levels of transcriptional regulation control glycolate metabolism in Paracoccus denitrificans. mBio (2024). DOI:10.1128/mbio.01524-24. [PMC11323563]
growth_records.snippet: Growth on glycolate (mu = 0.51 h-1) was faster than growth on glyoxylate (0.28 h-1)
growth_records.notes: WT mu=0.28 h-1 on 60 mM glyoxylate. td=0.693/0.28=2.48 h. No numeric uptake/secretion rate reported.

growth_records.medium: mineral salt medium (with TE3-Zn trace elements)
growth_records.cite: Lennart Schada von Borzyskowski et al. Multiple levels of transcriptional regulation control glycolate metabolism in Paracoccus denitrificans. mBio (2024). DOI:10.1128/mbio.01524-24. [PMC11323563]
growth_records.snippet: the growth rates on pyruvate (mu = 0.45 h-1) and glucose (mu = 0.38 h-1) were between these two values
growth_records.notes: WT mu=0.45 h-1 on 40 mM pyruvate. td=0.693/0.45=1.54 h.

growth_records.medium: mineral salt medium (with TE3-Zn trace elements)

up.method: luminescence-based assay; biomass-specific uptake rate calculated (Fig. 8d, WT vs dcceR)

growth_records.cite: Lennart Schada von Borzyskowski et al. Multiple levels of transcriptional regulation control glycolate metabolism in Paracoccus denitrificans. mBio (2024). DOI:10.1128/mbio.01524-24. [PMC11323563]
growth_records.snippet: the growth rates on pyruvate (mu = 0.45 h-1) and glucose (mu = 0.38 h-1)
growth_records.notes: WT mu=0.38 h-1 on 20 mM glucose. td=0.693/0.38=1.82 h. Biomass-specific glucose uptake rate measured (Fig. 8d) but numeric value not in text -> rate=null. Note: on glycolate+glucose mixes P. denitrificans co-assimilates simultaneously (no diauxie).

growth_records.strain: not stated (P. denitrificans; source data from Felgate et al. 2012)
growth_records.medium: defined chemostat medium, succinate carbon source + nitrate electron acceptor (high-copper, [Cu]=13 umol/L)

up.units: mmol/L (inflow feed concentration; per-biomass uptake rate not reported)
up.method: chemostat feed set-point (n_In=20 mmol/L for i=1)

sec.method: chemostat measurement, high-copper experiment; value quoted from Table 1 of Felgate et al. 2012

growth_records.cite: Woolfenden HC et al. Modeling the effect of copper availability on bacterial denitrification. MicrobiologyOpen (2013). DOI:10.1002/mbo3.111. [PMC3831637]
growth_records.snippet: The rates for high-copper ([Cu] = 13 umol/L) and low-copper ([Cu] = 0.5 umol/L) experiments involving P. denitrificans are quoted in Table 1 of Felgate et al. (2012)... The low rate (<0.1 umol N g-1 h-1) for the high-copper experiment shows
growth_records.notes: Chemostat steady state: growth_rate mu = dilution rate D = 0.05 h-1 (stated D=0.05 h-1 in nomenclature table). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.05 = 13.86 h (calculated). pH 7.5 stated for the calibration experiments; temperature not given for the cultivation (kinetic params obtained at pH~7, 25-35 C). Nitrate inflow n_In=20 mmol/L (i=1), 0 for others. N2O secretion rate <0.1 umol N gDW-1 h-1 is a MEASURED rate CITED FROM Felgate et al. (2012) Table 1, not measured in this modeling paper. No per-biomass succinate or nitrate uptake rate is reported here. High-copper condition = [Cu]=13 umol/L.

growth_records.strain: not stated (P. denitrificans; source data from Felgate et al. 2012)
growth_records.medium: defined chemostat medium, succinate carbon source + nitrate electron acceptor (low-copper, [Cu]=0.5 umol/L; ascorbate+BCA added to chelate extracellular copper)

up.units: mmol/L (inflow feed concentration; per-biomass uptake rate not reported)
up.method: chemostat feed set-point (n_In=20 mmol/L for i=1)

sec.method: chemostat measurement, low-copper experiment; value quoted from Felgate et al. 2012

growth_records.cite: Woolfenden HC et al. Modeling the effect of copper availability on bacterial denitrification. MicrobiologyOpen (2013). DOI:10.1002/mbo3.111. [PMC3831637]
growth_records.snippet: The sharp increase in production rate (1200 umol N g-1 h-1) in the low-copper experiment implies that the level of active Nos has dropped considerably... we use [Cu] = 0.5 umol/L for calibration purposes and use it to represent the copper-l
growth_records.notes: Chemostat steady state: mu = D = 0.05 h-1 (stated). doubling_time = ln2/0.05 = 13.86 h (calculated). N2O secretion rate 1200 umol N gDW-1 h-1 is a MEASURED rate CITED FROM Felgate et al. (2012), not measured in this modeling paper; contrasts with <0.1 umol N gDW-1 h-1 at high copper (13 umol/L). Low-copper condition = [Cu]=0.5 umol/L with ascorbate+BCA chelators. Nitrate inflow 20 mmol/L. pH 7.5. No per-biomass succinate/nitrate uptake rate reported. This modeling paper reports no primary experimental rate of its own beyond re-quoting Felgate et al. 2012.
